title: Parties, Roles and Responsibilities in Engineering Projects
description: Learn who participates in engineering projects, how responsibilities and authority are distributed, and why clear roles, interfaces and communication are essential for successful project execution.

sections.title: Learning Objectives
items: Identify the main parties commonly involved in engineering projects.
Explain the difference between a party, stakeholder, role, responsibility and authority.
Describe the responsibilities of the project owner, client, project manager, consultant, contractor, supplier and subcontractor.
Understand how responsibilities may differ depending on the project delivery model.
Explain why roles and decision authority should be clarified at the beginning of a project.
Recognize common responsibility gaps, overlaps and interface risks.
Understand how responsibility matrices and organization charts support project governance.
Apply role and responsibility analysis to practical engineering-project scenarios.

sections.title: Why Parties and Responsibilities Matter
content: Engineering projects are rarely completed by one person or one organization. They normally involve several parties that contribute technical knowledge, financing, equipment, labour, approvals and operational experience.

Each party enters the project with its own objectives, risks, obligations and expectations. The owner may focus on long-term value, the contractor on efficient delivery, the supplier on product performance and the operator on safe and reliable operation.
Project performance depends on how effectively these parties cooperate.

When roles, responsibilities or authority are unclear, important tasks may be delayed, duplicated or omitted. Disagreements may also arise about who should make a decision, perform additional work or pay for an unexpected cost.

sections.title: Party, Stakeholder, Role, Responsibility and Authority
content: A party is an individual or organization that participates formally in a project relationship. A party may be connected to the project through a contract, appointment, ownership interest or regulatory mandate.
A stakeholder is any person or organization that can influence the project or be affected by it. A stakeholder does not need to be a contractual party.
A role describes the function a person or organization performs in the project.
Responsibility describes the task, decision or result for which that person or organization is accountable.
Authority describes the formal right to approve work, commit funds, issue instructions, accept deliverables or make decisions.
These concepts are related but not identical. A person may have responsibility for preparing a recommendation without having authority to approve it.

sections.title: Example: Responsibility Without Authority
content: A project engineer is responsible for evaluating design changes.
The engineer can recommend whether a change should be accepted, but only the project manager has authority to approve changes that affect cost or schedule.
The engineer therefore has technical responsibility but not final commercial authority.

sections.title: The Project Owner
content: The project owner is the organization that initiates and ultimately owns the project outcome.
The owner normally defines the business need, provides funding and determines the strategic objectives of the project.
The owner may also approve the overall budget, major scope changes, major contracts and key project decisions.
In some projects, the owner and the client are the same organization. In others, the owner appoints another organization or department to act as the client or project representative.
The owner remains concerned with whether the completed project creates the expected long-term value.

sections.title: The Client or Customer
content: The client is the party that purchases or commissions the engineering work, equipment or project delivery.
The client defines requirements, communicates expectations, evaluates offers and enters agreements with consultants, contractors or suppliers.
The client is normally responsible for providing accurate information, access, approvals and decisions required for the project to proceed.
The client should also avoid giving informal instructions through unauthorized personnel.
A client may be the project owner, an operator acting on behalf of the owner or a main contractor purchasing work from a supplier.

sections.title: Example: Owner and Client
content: A shipping company owns a vessel and decides to install a new fuel-efficiency system.
The shipping company is the project owner.
Its technical management department may act as the client when purchasing engineering services and equipment from suppliers.

sections.title: The Project Sponsor
content: The project sponsor is a senior person who supports the project within the owner's or client's organization.
The sponsor helps secure funding, resolves high-level organizational problems and provides strategic direction.
The sponsor normally does not manage daily project activities.
Instead, the sponsor ensures that the project remains aligned with organizational objectives and receives the necessary management support.
The sponsor may also approve major deviations that exceed the project manager's authority.

sections.title: The Project Manager
content: The project manager is responsible for coordinating the project and guiding it toward its objectives.
Typical responsibilities include planning, organizing resources, monitoring progress, managing risks, coordinating stakeholders and controlling scope, cost, schedule and quality.
The project manager also ensures that decisions are made by the correct authority and that important issues are escalated when necessary.
A project manager does not personally perform every task. The role is to integrate the work of different disciplines, organizations and specialists.
The project manager's authority should be defined clearly, especially regarding budgets, changes, supplier instructions and contract commitments.

sections.title: Project Manager Does Not Mean Unlimited Authority
content: The project manager may coordinate the project without having authority to approve every change.
Financial limits, contractual requirements and governance procedures may require approval from the sponsor, owner or contract manager.
A project manager should understand both the authority granted to the role and the decisions that must be escalated.

sections.title: The Engineering Manager
content: The engineering manager coordinates the project's technical development.
The role may include managing engineering disciplines, approving technical methods, coordinating design reviews and ensuring that technical requirements are satisfied.
The engineering manager often works closely with the project manager, but the roles are different.
The engineering manager focuses primarily on the technical solution, while the project manager integrates technical, commercial, contractual and organizational considerations.
In smaller projects, one person may perform both roles.

sections.title: Discipline Engineers
content: Discipline engineers provide specialist knowledge within areas such as mechanical engineering, electrical engineering, automation, software, structural engineering or process engineering.
They may prepare calculations, specifications, drawings, technical evaluations and test procedures.
Discipline engineers are also responsible for identifying technical risks and interfaces related to their area.
They should communicate assumptions and changes that may affect other disciplines.
A technically correct decision within one discipline can still create problems if its effects on the rest of the project are ignored.

sections.title: Example: Multidisciplinary Consequences
content: A mechanical engineer selects a larger pump to increase capacity.
The larger pump may require more electrical power, stronger foundations, larger pipes and additional cooling.
The mechanical decision must therefore be coordinated with electrical, structural and process engineers.

sections.title: The Consultant
content: A consultant provides professional expertise to the owner, client or contractor.
Consultants may perform studies, design work, calculations, project management, procurement support, inspections or technical verification.
The consultant's responsibilities are defined by the consulting agreement.
A consultant may recommend a solution without having authority to commit the client.
The client should clarify whether the consultant may issue instructions, approve work or represent the client in contractual matters.

sections.title: Consultant Advice and Client Decisions
content: A consultant may provide expert advice, but the client normally remains responsible for business and contractual decisions unless authority has been formally delegated.
Both parties should understand whether the consultant is acting as an adviser, designer, project manager or authorized representative.

sections.title: The Main Contractor
content: The main contractor is responsible for delivering a defined part of the project under a contract with the client.
Depending on the contract, the contractor may be responsible for construction, installation, engineering, procurement, commissioning or complete project delivery.
The contractor must perform the agreed scope in accordance with the contract, technical requirements, schedule, quality standards and safety obligations.
The contractor is normally responsible for managing its own employees, suppliers and subcontractors.
The client generally communicates formal instructions through the contractual channels defined in the agreement.

sections.title: The Supplier
content: A supplier provides equipment, materials, systems or specialist services.
The supplier may be responsible for design, manufacturing, documentation, delivery, installation support, testing, training and warranties.
Supplier responsibility should be defined by the purchase order or contract.
The technical specification should clarify performance requirements, while the commercial terms should clarify price, delivery, liability, acceptance and payment.
A supplier should notify the customer when proposed changes may affect performance, compliance, interfaces or delivery.

sections.title: The Subcontractor
content: A subcontractor performs work for a main contractor rather than directly for the project owner or client.
The main contractor normally remains responsible to the client for subcontracted work.
The client may communicate with the subcontractor during daily work, but formal instructions should normally pass through the main contractor unless the contract states otherwise.
Direct instructions from the client to a subcontractor can create confusion about responsibility, price and schedule.

sections.title: Example: Direct Instruction to a Subcontractor
content: A client representative asks an electrical subcontractor to install an additional cable.
The subcontractor completes the work and later charges the main contractor.
The main contractor argues that it never approved the change.
The problem arose because the client bypassed the agreed contractual communication channel.

sections.title: The Operator and End User
content: The operator is responsible for using, maintaining or managing the completed system after handover.
The end user is the person or group that directly uses the system, facility or product.
Operators and end users often possess practical knowledge that is essential during requirement definition, design reviews, testing and training.
A project can satisfy the technical specification and still fail operationally if the solution is difficult to use, maintain or integrate into existing operations.
Early operator involvement can reduce this risk.

sections.title: Example: Operator Involvement
content: A new control system is technically functional but requires operators to complete several unnecessary manual steps.
Had the operators participated in the design review, the workflow could have been simplified before installation.

sections.title: Regulatory Authorities
content: Regulatory authorities ensure that the project complies with applicable laws, regulations, permits and safety requirements.
Authorities may review applications, inspect work, approve designs or issue certificates.
The project team should identify regulatory requirements early because approval processes may influence scope, design and schedule.
The authority does not normally manage the project, but its decisions may determine whether the project can proceed or operate legally.

sections.title: Independent Verification and Classification
content: Some engineering projects require independent verification by a third party.
This may include classification societies, certification bodies, technical inspectors, testing laboratories or independent engineers.
The independent party verifies compliance with specified rules, standards or contractual requirements.
Independent verification does not normally replace the contractor's responsibility for the quality and correctness of its work.
The exact role and authority of the verifier should be defined.

sections.title: The Contract Manager
content: The contract manager supports the administration and interpretation of project agreements.
Responsibilities may include monitoring contractual obligations, handling notices, evaluating changes, supporting negotiations and maintaining contract records.
The contract manager works with technical, commercial and project personnel.
The role is particularly important when decisions affect scope, payment, schedule, liability or risk allocation.
Contract management is not only performed when a dispute occurs. It should be integrated into daily project execution.

sections.title: The Procurement Manager
content: The procurement manager coordinates the process of obtaining goods and services from external parties.
Responsibilities may include supplier identification, tender preparation, bid evaluation, negotiation and purchase-order administration.
Procurement decisions should be based on more than purchase price.
Technical capability, delivery reliability, quality, commercial terms, lifecycle cost and risk should also be considered.
The procurement manager typically cooperates with engineers, project management, finance and contract management.

sections.title: Quality, Safety and Risk Roles
content: Quality personnel help ensure that processes and deliverables comply with specified requirements.
Safety personnel identify hazards, support risk assessments and monitor compliance with safety procedures.
Risk managers or project personnel responsible for risk coordination maintain the risk process and support risk reviews.
These roles provide specialist support, but they do not remove responsibility from the people performing and managing the work.
For example, a safety adviser supports the project, but every participant remains responsible for working safely.

sections.title: Finance and Project Control
content: Finance and project-control personnel support budgeting, forecasting, cost monitoring, progress measurement and reporting.
They help the project manager understand whether the project is performing according to plan.
Project control may also monitor commitments, invoices, earned progress, schedule performance and expected final cost.
These functions provide information for decision-making but normally do not own the technical scope.
Accurate reporting depends on timely information from engineers, contractors and project managers.

sections.title: Internal and External Parties
content: Internal parties belong to the owner's, client's or contractor's own organization.
External parties include consultants, suppliers, contractors, authorities and other independent organizations.
Internal parties may still have different priorities and reporting lines.
For example, operations may prioritize reliability, finance may prioritize investment cost and engineering may prioritize technical performance.
The project team must manage both organizational and contractual interfaces.

sections.title: Project Governance
content: Project governance defines how the project is directed, controlled and supervised.
It establishes decision levels, reporting lines, approval limits and escalation procedures.
Governance helps ensure that major decisions are made by people with the appropriate authority and information.
A governance structure may include the project sponsor, steering committee, project manager and specialist approval functions.
Governance should be proportionate to the project's size, complexity and risk.

sections.title: Example: Escalation of a Major Change
content: A project manager has authority to approve changes up to EUR 50,000.
A proposed design change is expected to cost EUR 200,000.
The project manager should evaluate the change and prepare a recommendation, but the final decision must be escalated to the authorized management level.

sections.title: Organization Charts
content: A project organization chart shows the formal structure of the project.
It may identify project leadership, engineering disciplines, commercial functions, contractors and reporting relationships.
The chart helps participants understand where they belong in the project and who they report to.
However, an organization chart does not normally describe detailed responsibilities or approval authority.
It should therefore be supported by role descriptions and responsibility matrices.

sections.title: Responsibility Matrices
content: A responsibility matrix connects project activities or deliverables with the parties responsible for them.
One common model is the RACI matrix.
RACI stands for Responsible, Accountable, Consulted and Informed.
Responsible identifies the person or party performing the work.
Accountable identifies the person who owns the result and approves completion.
Consulted identifies people whose input is required.
Informed identifies people who should receive information about the activity or decision.

sections.title: Example: RACI for a Design Change
content: The discipline engineer may be Responsible for evaluating the technical change.
The project manager may be Accountable for coordinating the complete change evaluation.
The contract manager and operator may be Consulted.
The project sponsor may be Informed, or may become Accountable if the value exceeds the project manager's authority.

sections.title: Too Many Accountable Parties
content: An activity should normally have one clearly identified accountable person.
When several people are described as equally accountable, final ownership may become unclear.
Multiple specialists may contribute, but one role should own the final decision or deliverable.

sections.title: Responsibility Gaps
content: A responsibility gap exists when no party is clearly assigned to perform or own an activity.
Gaps often occur at interfaces between contracts, departments or disciplines.
Examples include missing responsibility for temporary power, software integration, foundation design or operating documentation.
Responsibility gaps may remain unnoticed until execution or commissioning.
The project team should review scope boundaries and interfaces early.

sections.title: Responsibility Overlaps
content: A responsibility overlap exists when two or more parties believe they are responsible for the same activity.
Overlaps may lead to duplicated work, conflicting decisions or unnecessary cost.
They may also cause disputes if both parties expect payment for similar work.
Overlaps are not always harmful if coordination is clear, but ownership and decision authority must be defined.
The project team should distinguish between cooperation and duplicated accountability.

sections.title: Example: Responsibility Gap at an Interface
content: A supplier provides a new machine, while a contractor prepares the foundation.
The supplier assumes the contractor will design the anchor bolts.
The contractor assumes the supplier will provide the complete anchoring design.
The gap is discovered only when the machine arrives on site.

sections.title: Authority to Issue Instructions
content: Project contracts often identify specific people who may issue formal instructions.
This protects both parties from unauthorized commitments.
Employees may discuss technical matters without having authority to change scope, price or schedule.
Before acting on an instruction, the receiving party should determine whether the instruction comes from an authorized representative.
When authority is unclear, the issue should be clarified and documented.

sections.title: Informal Instructions
content: Informal discussions are useful for coordination, but they should not be treated automatically as formal changes.
Instructions affecting scope, cost, schedule, performance or contractual obligations should be confirmed through the agreed process.
Acting on an unauthorized instruction may create disputes about payment and responsibility.

sections.title: Delegation
content: Delegation occurs when a person with authority assigns responsibility or decision rights to another person.
Delegation should be clear regarding scope, limits and duration.
A project manager may delegate technical approval within a discipline while retaining responsibility for overall project coordination.
Delegation does not always remove the original manager's accountability.
The delegated person must also receive sufficient information, competence and resources.

sections.title: Communication Between Parties
content: Effective project communication depends on clear channels, timing and purpose.
Daily coordination may occur directly between engineers, supervisors and suppliers.
Formal contractual communication may need to pass through named representatives.
The project should distinguish between technical discussion, information sharing, approval and contractual instruction.
Meeting minutes, decision logs and action lists help confirm what was discussed and who is responsible for the next step.

sections.title: Meetings and Decision-Making
content: Project meetings should have a defined purpose, appropriate participants and clear follow-up.
Typical meetings include progress meetings, design reviews, risk reviews, interface meetings and commercial meetings.
Participants should know whether the meeting is intended to exchange information, develop recommendations or make decisions.
Important decisions should identify the decision-maker, basis, date and required actions.
A meeting does not create clear accountability unless decisions and actions are documented.

sections.title: Role Conflicts
content: Role conflict occurs when a person or organization faces incompatible expectations.
For example, a consultant may be asked to provide independent verification while also being responsible for the original design.
A project manager may experience pressure to reduce cost while maintaining schedule and quality.
Role conflicts should be recognized and managed through clear governance, transparency and appropriate separation of duties.
Unmanaged conflicts can weaken trust and decision quality.

sections.title: Different Project Delivery Models
content: Roles and responsibilities depend partly on how the project is organized contractually.
In a traditional model, the client may appoint a consultant to complete the design and then contract separately with a construction contractor.
In a design-and-build model, one contractor may be responsible for both design and execution.
In an engineering, procurement and construction model, the contractor may deliver a complete facility or system.
The project team must understand which responsibilities remain with the owner and which are transferred to other parties.

sections.title: Example: Design Responsibility
content: In one project, the client provides a complete design and the contractor builds according to it.
In another project, the contractor is responsible for developing the design from performance requirements.
The contractor's design responsibility is much greater in the second project, even though both projects may involve similar construction work.

sections.title: Transferred Responsibility Does Not Mean No Involvement
content: A client may transfer design or construction responsibility to a contractor through the contract.
However, the client may still need to provide information, review documents, make timely decisions and coordinate with operators or authorities.
The client should not interfere in a way that creates unclear responsibility.
At the same time, the client should maintain sufficient oversight to protect project objectives.
The balance between control and transferred responsibility should be understood by both parties.

sections.title: Responsibility for Information
content: Project parties often depend on information supplied by others.
The client may provide site data, operating conditions or existing drawings.
The contractor may provide design documents, calculations and progress information.
The supplier may provide equipment data and interface requirements.
Each party should clarify what information must be provided, when it is required and whether the receiving party must verify it.
Incorrect or late information can affect cost, schedule and technical performance.

sections.title: Responsibility for Approvals
content: Projects may require internal, client, regulatory and third-party approvals.
The project plan should define who prepares documents, who reviews them and who gives final approval.
Approval does not always transfer responsibility for the original work.
For example, client approval of a contractor's drawing may not remove the contractor's responsibility for design correctness.
The contractual meaning of review and approval should therefore be understood.

sections.title: Approval Is Not Always Acceptance of Responsibility
content: A party reviewing or approving a document may only be confirming that it is acceptable for the project to proceed.
The creator of the document may remain responsible for errors, compliance and technical performance.
The effect of approval depends on the agreement and the wording of the approval process.

sections.title: Responsibility During Changes
content: When a change is proposed, several roles may become involved.
Engineering evaluates technical feasibility and consequences.
Planning assesses schedule effects.
Commercial and project-control personnel evaluate cost.
Contract management determines contractual rights and procedures.
The authorized decision-maker approves or rejects the change.
The change should not be implemented before responsibilities and authority are clarified.

sections.title: Example: Change Responsibility
content: A vessel operator requests relocation of a control panel.
The engineer evaluates technical suitability.
The contractor estimates additional work.
The project manager assesses schedule impact.
The contract manager checks whether the request is outside the original scope.
The authorized client representative decides whether to approve the change.

sections.title: Responsibility During Handover
content: Handover transfers the completed system from the project organization to the owner or operator.
Responsibilities may include completing testing, correcting defects, delivering documentation, providing training and transferring spare parts.
The receiving organization should verify that agreed handover requirements are satisfied.
The project team should also clarify which responsibilities continue after handover, such as warranties, maintenance support and defect correction.
Unclear handover responsibilities can delay operational use and final payment.

sections.title: Expert Insight
content: Many engineering-project failures are not caused by a lack of technical skill. They result from unclear ownership, weak interfaces, delayed decisions or unauthorized instructions.
Strong project teams make responsibilities visible. They clarify who performs the work, who owns the result, who must be consulted and who has authority to decide.
Responsibility should be reviewed whenever scope, organization, contracts or project phases change.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
items: Assuming that job titles automatically define project authority.
Giving a person responsibility without sufficient decision-making authority.
Allowing several parties to believe they are equally accountable for the same decision.
Failing to assign responsibility for technical and organizational interfaces.
Allowing unauthorized personnel to issue instructions to contractors or suppliers.
Confusing technical approval with commercial authorization.
Assuming that client review removes contractor responsibility.
Failing to involve operators and end users during design and testing.
Using organization charts without detailed role descriptions.
Not updating responsibilities when the project scope or organization changes.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
items: Engineering projects involve several parties with different objectives, obligations and risks.
A party is formally involved in the project, while a stakeholder may influence or be affected by it without having a contract.
A role describes a function, responsibility describes ownership of work or results, and authority describes the right to make decisions.
The project owner, client, sponsor, project manager, engineers, consultants, contractors, suppliers and operators have different responsibilities.
Responsibility and authority must be aligned to avoid confusion and delay.
Organization charts show reporting relationships, while responsibility matrices clarify ownership of activities and decisions.
Responsibility gaps and overlaps commonly occur at technical, organizational and contractual interfaces.
Formal instructions should be issued only by authorized representatives.
Roles and responsibilities depend on the project's contractual and organizational model.
Responsibilities should be reviewed throughout the project lifecycle, especially when changes occur.

sections.title: Reflection
questions: What is the difference between being responsible for evaluating a decision and having authority to approve it?
Why might a project fail if the operator is not involved during design and testing?
How can direct communication between a client and a subcontractor create contractual problems?
What information should be included in a responsibility matrix?
Why should one person normally be clearly accountable for each major activity or deliverable?
How may design responsibility differ between a traditional project and a design-and-build project?
Who should be involved when a technical change affects scope, cost and schedule?
What responsibility gaps could occur when one supplier delivers equipment and another contractor performs installation?
